item.title: Chains in episodes of autocratization (Preparing for Submission)
item.description: Are there patterns in the sequences of institutional change when democracies autocratize? If so, are such patterns distinct for democracies that transition into authoritarianism vs. those that avert democratic breakdown? And do such orders vary by the underlying conditions of autocratizing countries? The Episodes of Regime Transformation (ERT) dataset provides a global sample of all 69 autocratization episodes between 1900-2021. Using this data and pairwise domination analysis, we describe the general order of reforms in 31 variables which make up different types of accountability mechanisms constraining the government. The paper empirically demonstrates how different aspects of accountability decline during episodes of autocratization in democracies distinguishing between cases in which democracy ultimately breaks down or those in which breakdown is averted. Our findings suggest that the general institutional decay starts with horizontal accountability, followed by the decline in diagonal accountability, and finally, vertical accountability. This pattern becomes more apparent in the context of low democratic stock and the third wave of autocratization. The study is of high relevance not only for improving academic knowledge but also for policymakers and practitioners seeking to counteract further autocratization.

item.title: Crisis of Democracy: Targeted Protest and Affective Polarization (Preparing for Submission)
item.description: Political protests increasingly play an important role in shaping politics in democracies. What political consequences did this new global protest cycle bring about within democratic countries with unrest? Drawing on social identity theory, I argue that protest events, which targeted the ruling party or leaders, would serve as a focal event that may enhance voters’ attachment to partisan. Targeted protests provide open information containing a specific political message about targeted candidates or parties to the public. Accordingly, exposure to protests may activate and reinforce pre-existing partisan identities —including positive and negative— and ultimately triggers affective polarization at the mass level. To examine this argument, I use the cross-national survey data to test whether protests targeting the ruling party increase the level of mass polarization among 30 electoral democracies between 1996 and 2018. The findings have implications for understanding the impact of protests on voting behavior and the process of polarization in the context of widespread protests in democracies.

item.title: Stability and Change in the Foreign Aid Policy: The Role of Institutional Constraints (Working Paper)
item.description: Although foreign policy is often characterized by continuity, major reorientations still happen. Extant explanations focus on various shocks as triggers of change. However, literature has yet fully identified the underlying institutional conditions, making a significant foreign policy change likely. This study examines how institutional constraints in various segments of domestic society affect foreign policy change. We analyze the annual change in the official development assistance (ODA) profile among the OECD member states from 1987 to 2016 and found that the number of veto players and independent merit-based bureaucracy substantially affects overseas developmental aid policy stability. We further examine the shift in ODA profile in major donor countries and illustrate how institutional constraints have delayed the ODA profile change. The paper adds to the literature on a foreign policy change by identifying domestic institutional conditions in the donor countries as a cause of change.
